relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Moses carries his final words through the whole assembly.
At one hundred twenty years, he names the limit he cannot cross.
Joshua will lead the people beyond the river.
The coming campaign is interpreted through Sihon and Og.
The people are told to carry out the inherited command.
Courage rests on presence that does not abandon.
Joshua is strengthened publicly before Israel.
The leader is told that YHWH goes before him.
Moses writes Torah and entrusts it to priests and elders.
The year of release becomes the time for renewed public hearing.
Torah must be read aloud before the whole people.
Men, women, children, and immigrants all stand inside the listening community.
Children who did not witness the past receive the words through hearing.
Moses and Joshua stand together at the tent as succession approaches.
The cloud marks Divine presence at the threshold.
The future failure of the people is described through a sexual betrayal metaphor.
Catastrophe will feel like abandonment and hidden face.
The rhetoric intensifies the experience of estrangement.
A song is written so memory can speak when institutions fail.
Abundance becomes dangerous when satisfaction forgets covenant.
The song remains in descendants' mouths as a witness against forgetting.
Moses teaches the song on the day it is written.
Joshua receives courage and the promise of presence.
Moses completes the written Torah.
The Levites receive responsibility for the scroll.
The Torah rests beside the ark as a continuing witness.
Moses names resistance without idealizing the people he loves.
Elders and officers gather beneath heaven and earth as witnesses.
Moses anticipates corruption after his death, proving the covenant cannot depend upon his presence alone.
The assembly receives the completed song.

contemplative_note: Deuteronomy 31 prepares for leadership after the founder. Joshua is commissioned publicly, Torah is written and read to everyone, and a song is placed in communal memory. Healthy succession does not make one person indispensable; it makes wisdom accessible when that person is gone.
